metadata.summary: Formal termination process for a project: obtain written deliverable acceptance, release resources, close contracts, capture lessons learned, archive documents, and transition to operations.

content.title: Project Closure
content.summary: Formal termination process for a project: obtain written deliverable acceptance, release resources, close contracts, capture lessons learned, archive documents, and transition to operations. Without a closure event, projects fade without official acceptance, resources stay allocated to finished work, and knowledge walks out with the team.

why.p: Informal endings leave ambiguous completion status, open vendor contracts (ongoing costs, liabilities), unarchived documentation, and no knowledge transfer. Formal closure enforces a hard boundary that unlocks people for new work, triggers financial reconciliation, and seeds future projects with lessons. Get acceptance in writing — verbal "looks good" does not hold at invoice time.

list.item: End of any contracted or fixed-bid engagement requiring formal acceptance.
Cancelled or descoped projects (closure must capture salvage value and lessons).
Phase-gated programs at major milestone transitions (Phase 1 → Phase 2 closure).
Internal projects transitioning from build to operate (handover to ops/support).
Continuous-flow or product teams — no closure event; only release boundaries.
Solo throwaway prototypes — a DONE.md line is enough.
Projects still in execution; premature closure loses incomplete deliverables.

section.name: step-1-verify-deliverable-acceptance
section.p: Obtain written acceptance for every deliverable before declaring closure; verbal confirmation does not hold at invoice time.

rule.statement: Obtain written acceptance for every deliverable before declaring closure; verbal confirmation does not hold at invoice time.
rule.rationale: Ambiguous acceptance is the most common source of post-project invoice disputes and scope creep into "maintenance" work that was never bought.

section.p: For each deliverable, confirm: all requirements met, testing complete, documentation delivered, sign-off obtained in writing.
section.reference: Per-deliverable acceptance form with criteria table and signature block.

rule.statement: Process all financial, resource, and contract closure actions within the closure window; do not leave open POs or un-released team members.
rule.rationale: Open contracts accumulate charges. Resources "stuck" on finished projects block new assignments and frustrate individuals.

list.item: Financial: final invoices, close POs, return unused budget, reconcile costs.
Resource: release team members, return equipment, revoke access/permissions, notify resource managers.
Contract: final vendor payments, warranty/support handover, termination letters, archive agreements.

section.name: step-3-lessons-learned

rule.statement: Run the lessons-learned session before the team disperses, not after; memory and candor both degrade within days.
rule.rationale: Post-dispersal sessions yield low attendance and sanitized data. Lessons captured in flight are richer and more actionable.

section.p: Document what worked and what did not; store in the organizational knowledge base. Capture both successes and failures — teams that log only failures stop contributing honestly.

section.name: step-4-archive-project-documents

rule.statement: Archive documents into named categories (planning, execution, technical, contracts, closure) with a manifest so they can be found in 12 months.
rule.rationale: Unstructured "shared drive" archives are unfindable; without a manifest, future auditors and PMs have no entry point.

section.reference: Shell script to bundle the archive with per-category folders and SHA-256 manifest.

section.name: step-5-write-final-project-report
section.p: Document project completion covering: executive summary, deliverables, schedule/budget/scope/quality performance, key accomplishments, challenges and resolutions, lessons-learned summary, transition information, and acknowledgments.
section.reference: Final report skeleton with all required sections.

section.name: step-6-transition-to-operations

rule.statement: Hand operations a working runbook on day -7, not day 0; shadow week lets them surface gaps before you are gone.
rule.rationale: Day-0 handovers leave the ops team inheriting an unsupportable system with no one to ask questions.

section.p: Handover items: system access, documentation, support tickets, monitoring, L1/L2/L3 contacts, escalation matrix, scheduled maintenance.
section.reference: Operations handover template covering all required sections.

rule.statement: Celebrate visibly and name individual contributions; do not cut this step under schedule pressure.
rule.rationale: Skipping recognition is the leading cause of post-project demoralization, increasing attrition risk on the next engagement.

list.item: Normal — objectives met: full closure process.
Early (success) — objectives met ahead of schedule: accelerated closure.
Early (cancelled) — stopped before completion: document reasons, recover value.
Integration — absorbed into operations: transition-focused closure.

example.title: Customer Portal v2.0 normal closure
example.note: 6 months, $150k: final UAT sign-off obtained from business sponsor; production confirmed stable after 2-week soak; support team trained; documentation handed over; final cost $147,500 (under budget); 5 lessons captured; team celebration lunch held; final report distributed; project marked closed in system.

example.title: Cancelled Mobile App Prototype closure
example.note: Strategic pivot, 30% complete: documented work completed and reasons; lessons learned on prototyping approach; recovered reusable design assets; released resources to other projects; negotiated early vendor contract termination; archived all documentation; final report with cancellation rationale — value recovered even from incomplete work.

antipattern.description: No formal acceptance — assuming silence means approved.
antipattern.reason: Closure without written acceptance leaves deliverable status ambiguous; six months later this becomes an invoice dispute with no documentation to support the PM's position.

antipattern.description: Resources not released — people stuck on "finished" projects.
antipattern.reason: Trapped resources block new work and signal to team members that their time is not valued, increasing turnover risk.

antipattern.description: Contracts left open — ongoing vendor charges accumulate.
antipattern.reason: Open POs continue to accrue costs and legal liabilities long after work stopped; finance discovers this in quarterly audits, not daily operations.

antipattern.description: No lessons captured — knowledge walks out the door with the team.
antipattern.reason: Without documented lessons, the next project repeats the same mistakes. Institutional knowledge is the only free input to future project success.

antipattern.description: No celebration — team feels unappreciated after sustained effort.
antipattern.reason: Skipping recognition signals that delivery is expected but not valued, the leading cultural factor in team attrition after project completion.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A subagent excels at the closure-administration grind: assemble the closure checklist from project artefacts, generate the deliverable acceptance form pre-filled per item, draft the final report from status reports + change register + EVM, package documents into a structured archive (with a MANIFEST.json), and produce a per-stakeholder closure communication. Humans drive acceptance signoffs, lessons-learned facilitation, and recognition. Run the agent at the end of execution, then again 30 days post-go-live for benefits-tracking handoff.

Recommended subagents: faion-pm-agent — owns closure checklist; drafts final report and handover doc. sdd-execution quality gate — verifies acceptance criteria are met against the original specs. improver agent (skill) — facilitates lessons-learned and writes follow-up actions. faion-communicator — drafts per-audience closure communications (sponsor, team, ops).

list.item: Run lessons-learned BEFORE the team disperses, not after; resentment fades but so does memory.
Get acceptance signoff in writing per deliverable — verbal "looks good" never holds up at invoice time.
Capture both successes and failures; teams that only log failures stop contributing honestly.
Hand operations a working runbook on day -7, not day 0; let them break it during shadow week.
Schedule a 30-day and 90-day post-closure review to catch latent defects and benefits drift.
Celebrate visibly — sponsor email, team lunch, named recognition; budget it before kickoff.
LLMs draft glowing closure reports by default; explicitly require "challenges" and "what failed" sections with cited examples.
Agent-extracted metrics from inconsistent status reports drift; require source row references for every number.
Do not let the agent draft acceptance signoffs as if signed; the form must be human-signed (digital signature ok).
Lessons-learned summaries by LLMs sound generic; have the agent group quotes verbatim before paraphrasing.
Token budget: closure reports across a multi-month project exceed context — chunk by phase, then synthesize.
Human-in-loop checkpoints: (1) acceptance signoffs, (2) ops-team review of handover, (3) sponsor signoff on final report, (4) archival manifest verification.

content.reference: Signed-off checklist covering all seven closure areas.

template.purpose: Signed-off checklist covering acceptance, admin, resource release, knowledge, transition, and communication.
Per-deliverable acceptance form with criteria table, outstanding items, and signature block.
Operations handover template: system overview, docs index, support tiers, known issues, monitoring, scheduled maintenance.
Final project report skeleton: executive summary, deliverables, schedule/budget/scope/quality performance, lessons, transition info.
Shell script to bundle project archive with per-category folders and SHA-256 manifest.

see-also.link: PMI PMBOK Guide 7th ed., Delivery Performance Domain — Project Closure.
